step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("current status of peanut allergy vaccine development")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

We previously concluded "there is no approved vaccine" for peanut allergy. New evidence confirms that while there is still no official license for a preventative vaccine, research into immunotherapy—including oral immunotherapy (OIT)—has advanced significantly. These treatments do not function like traditional vaccines (which confer immunity against pathogens); rather, they aim to modify the immune response so that patients tolerate peanuts. Some experimental phases involving peptides or modified proteins exist, but none have achieved full approval as preventive measures. Thus, despite ongoing innovation, there is no established, widely accepted vaccine for peanut allergy.
I fully agree with the prior conclusion. The retrieved evidence reinforces that no preventative peanut allergy vaccine is currently approved or clinically standard. Instead, management focuses on early introduction of peanuts and immunotherapy techniques aimed at building tolerance—not preventing the allergy through immune activation akin to infection-preventing vaccines.
Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$

We previously concluded "there is no approved vaccine" for peanut allergy. New evidence confirms that while there is still no official license for a preventative vaccine, research into immunotherapy—including oral immunotherapy (OIT)—has advanced significantly. These treatments do not function like traditional vaccines (which confer immunity against pathogens); rather, they aim to modify the immune response so that patients tolerate peanuts. Some experimental phases involving peptides or modified proteins exist, but none have achieved full approval as preventive measures. Thus, despite ongoing innovation, there is no established, widely accepted vaccine for peanut allergy.
I fully agree with the prior conclusion. The retrieved evidence reinforces that no preventative peanut allergy vaccine is currently approved or clinically standard. Instead, management focuses on early introduction of peanuts and immunotherapy techniques aimed at building tolerance—not preventing the allergy through immune activation akin to infection-preventing vaccines.
Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$
